mappings.rationaleAI: The transition to mechanized factory manufacturing corresponds to changes in production techniques (A.4), mass production organizing labor by product (A.5), machines replacing labor (A.6), and psychological/social effects of rationalized work (C.1).
Agricultural cultivation from Neolithic origins corresponds to communal organization required by agriculture development (A.1.b) and organization of agricultural labor in the ancient world (A.2.c).
Neolithic farming origins and subsequent agricultural developments correspond to specialization required by agriculture (A.1.b) and organization of agricultural labor (A.2.c).
Robots displacing workers and raising automation concerns directly correspond to machines as replacements for labor (A.6) and psychological/social effects of automation (C.1).
Hierarchical social stratification by economic position, education, and occupation corresponds to the effect of social classes on the organization of labor (A.2.b).

Administration of organizations through planning, organizing, directing, and controlling corresponds to the application of scientific methods to managerial functions (B) and operations research (B.1).
Prehistoric tool use, hunting, and gathering correspond to the organization of work in the prehistoric world (A.1) including initial division of labor.

The transition to sedentary agriculture and social stratification corresponds to communal specialization required by agriculture and metallurgy (A.1.b) and theories of civilization's development (A.2.a).
Systematic design processes solving technological problems correspond to systems engineering (B.2) and the utilization of scientific knowledge in planning complex systems.

Class struggle between bourgeoisie and proletariat and critique of alienated labor correspond to the effect of social classes on labor organization (A.2.b) and psychological/social aspects of mass production (C.1).
Systematic knowledge-building through testable hypotheses provides the foundation for the application of scientific methods to managerial functions (B).
The period 1500-1800 with mercantile capitalism and accelerating technological change corresponds to changes in production techniques from the 16th to 18th century (A.4).
The European movable-type printing press represents an innovation relevant to organization of work in the medieval world through craft guild production (A.3).
The construction of buildings connects to the organization of labor for large-scale construction in the ancient world (A.2.e).
Agricultural management of livestock from Neolithic domestication corresponds to communal organization required by developing agricultural specialization (A.1.b).
Forced industrialization via five-year plans and the Gulag labor camp system represent extreme forms of mass production organization (A.5) with severe psychological and social consequences (C.1).
Social science disciplines studying human societies and behavior provide methodological foundations for operations research and scientific management (B.1).

The state article covers theories of civilization's development and the origin of hierarchical organization (A.2.a), addressing scholarly explanations of how early states emerged from stateless societies through economic and coercive stratification of labor.
Intensifying economic interdependence and global supply chains reshape mass production and the organization of labor by product across international boundaries (A.5).
